title: Regulation of pro-inflammatory responses in a simple in vitro model of chronic oxidant exposure in COPD
author: Tarhini, Fawziye
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Purpose: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) is caused by long-term exposure to pollutants such as cigarette smoke (CS) that stimulates an inflammatory response and produces oxidants that cause oxidative stress (OS) which is considered the main cause of COPD. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is one of many oxidants identified to be elevated in the breath condensates of COPD patients resulting in OS and it has been found to be associated with enhanced inflammation. Chronic inflammation is another key feature of COPD that persists long after smoking cessation and is driven by multiple pro-inflammatory cytokines and OS. Inflammation and OS can lead to permanent epigenetic modulation patterns of genes that are correlated with COPD. Protease-activated receptor 2 (PAR2) has been identified as a critical and key protease sensor and driver of pathology in several inflammatory diseases including asthma, but the roles of this receptor in COPD are still unclear. This study aimed to understand how chronic exposure to H2O2 alone or in combination with other inflammatory mediators influences cell response relevant to COPD lung pathogenesis, and whether these challenges alter DNA methylation levels of the PAR2 promoter region, to reveal potential implications for, and mechanistic insights into, COPD development. Methods: H2O2 was used as a model oxidant to establish an in vitro chronic oxidative stress system. BEAS-2B cells were exposed chronically in a repeated exposure manner to H2O2 for 2 h/day for 3 days at different concentrations, alone or in combination with inflammatory mediators (LPS, TGF-β and TNF-α; constant exposure for 48 h). Effects of pre-treatment with a selective IKK-2 inhibitor (TPCA-1) was investigated to assess the contribution of the NF-κB pathway. Cell viability was assessed by MTT assay. Cytokines were measured by ELISA, intracellular ROS production was detected by 2',7'-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (CMH2DCFDA) assay and mRNA transcript levels were assayed by qPCR. Model development experiments were performed using primary human bronchial epithelial cells from healthy and COPD donors (HBEC/COPD-HBEC). Methprimer software was used to predict the CpG islands/sites of the F2RL1 promoter region, and TFBS and JASPAR software were used to identify potential motifs and their binding sites. DNA from treated and untreated cells was isolated and bisulphite treated. Bisulphite-specific PCR was performed and PCR products were purified and sequenced, prior to quantitative analysis (University of Glasgow Polyomics). Results: Cells tolerated a repeated exposure of H2O2 up to 15 μM in BEAS-2B, 25 μM in HBEC and 50 μM in COPD-HBEC alone or in combination with 10 ng/ml of TGF-β and TNF-α or 500 ng/ml of LPS with only moderate impact on viability (MTT), and no effect on visual appearance/morphology. Intracellular ROS were significantly elevated in the presence of either LPS or H2O2, with an additive effect of combined treatment. H2O2 stimulated modest or no release of IL-8 and IL-6 alone but synergistically enhanced LPS-induced IL-8 and IL-6.
